task: I’m trying to figure out whether a machine issue is actually fixed or just behaving for the moment, and I don’t want to trust a single clean run. What’s a solid way to verify it under conditions that would actually flush out a weak spot?

turns.agent_response:
Use **repeatable verification under the original trigger conditions**, not just a single good run.

## Solid verification method

### 1) Recreate the original failure condition
Test it under the same:
- load
- temperature
- speed
- startup/shutdown state
- operator step
- vibration / pressure / voltage conditions

If the fault used to show up only under a specific condition, that condition has to be part of the test.

### 2) Run it more than once
A single clean run proves very little.  
Do at least:
- **2–3 consecutive runs** under the same condition
- enough duration to cover the usual failure window

If it used to fail after 20 minutes, don’t stop at 5.

### 3) Watch the original symptom, not just “does it work”
Check:
- the exact output that failed
- the alarm code
- the measured value
- the transition point where it used to go bad

You want to see whether the weak spot is still there, even if it hasn’t failed yet.

### 4) Vary one stress factor at a time
After the baseline repeat:
- increase load
- change temperature
- repeat the startup sequence
- introduce the same operator step
- run to the same cycle count

Only change one thing per run so you know what the fix can tolerate.

### 5) Look for early warning signs
Even if it doesn’t fail, check for:
- drift
- delay
- extra noise
- higher current draw
- temperature rise
- intermittent alarms
- trend changes in logs

A fix that “passes” but shows drift is not fully verified.

---

## Simple pass/fail rule
Call it verified only if:
- the original trigger condition is reproduced,
- the machine survives **multiple consecutive runs**,
- and the original failure mode does **not** reappear or drift toward failure.

If it passes once but you didn’t hit the real trigger, it’s not verified — it’s just a lucky run.

## Best practice
Keep a short verification record:
- test condition
- run count
- duration
- observed result
- any drift or warning signs

If you want, I can turn this into a **verification checklist** you can use on the floor.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right idea, but it’s still a little generic.

What I’d want tightened up:
- define the actual failure envelope, not just “original trigger conditions”
- include a **negative control** if you can, so you know the test setup itself isn’t masking the fault
- add **acceptance criteria** up front: what counts as drift, what counts as pass, what’s a hard fail
- if it’s intermittent, extend the dwell time past the first clean run; one good cycle means basically nothing

I’d also separate:
1) **reproduction test** — prove the fault still appears when stressed the old way
2) **verification test** — prove the fix survives repeated stress across the expected range
3) **soak / endurance** — prove it doesn’t degrade after time, heat, vibration, whatever actually matters

If you want, make it into a one-page checklist with columns for condition / run count / measured parameter / limit / result. That’s the part I’d actually use.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **verification checklist** that separates **reproduction**, **verification**, and **soak/endurance**, with a **negative control** and **up-front acceptance criteria**.

---

# Machine fix verification checklist

## 1) Define the failure envelope up front
Write the exact envelope you care about:

- **Condition(s):** load / temp / speed / startup state / vibration / voltage / pressure / operator step
- **Failure mode:** exact output, alarm, measured deviation, or stop
- **Failure window:** when it usually appears
- **Measured parameter:** the value that proves pass/fail
- **Limit:** the threshold or range
- **Hard fail:** what immediately ends the test

If you can’t define this, you can’t verify the fix.

---

## 2) Reproduction test
**Purpose:** prove the fault still appears when stressed the old way.

### Test
- Recreate the **same failure envelope**
- Run the machine under the **original trigger**
- Keep all other variables as unchanged as possible

### Pass / fail
- **Pass:** the original fault does **not** appear under the known trigger
- **Fail:** the original fault appears again
- **Inconclusive:** trigger not actually reached

### Minimum
- Do **2 runs** under the same trigger if the fault is intermittent
- If it used to fail after a certain time, run at least that long

---

## 3) Verification test
**Purpose:** prove the fix survives repeated stress across the expected range.

### Test
- Repeat the same condition across the expected operating range
- Change **one variable at a time** if you’re checking margin
- Keep the fix under the same stress that exposed the issue

### Acceptance criteria
Define these before running:
- no recurrence of the original fault
- no drift beyond limit
- no new alarms
- measured parameter stays within spec
- no abnormal heat, noise, delay, current draw, or vibration

### Pass / fail
- **Pass:** all runs stay inside limits and no original fault returns
- **Fail:** any run crosses the limit or the fault reappears

---

## 4) Soak / endurance test
**Purpose:** prove it doesn’t degrade after time, heat, vibration, or cycle count.

### Test
- Run for the **full time/cycle window that matters**
- Hold the machine under the stress that used to expose the weakness
- Don’t stop at the first clean cycle

### Pass / fail
- **Pass:** stable through the full dwell/run window
- **Fail:** drift, delay, alarm, or failure appears later in the run

### Rule
If the issue is intermittent, **one clean cycle is not enough**.  
You need the time window where it used to fail.

---

## 5) Negative control
**Purpose:** make sure the test setup itself isn’t masking the fault.

### Test
- Run one condition where the fault **should not** appear
- Compare behavior to the stressed condition
- Confirm the setup can still distinguish good vs bad

### Pass / fail
- **Pass:** fault is absent in the control condition and present only where expected
- **Fail:** the test setup hides the fault or makes everything look good

If you can’t do a true negative control, note that the result is weaker.

---

## 6) Stop / hold rules
Stop and hold if:
- the original fault returns
- a new alarm appears
- the measured parameter crosses the limit
- the machine behaves differently in a way that could be early drift
- the test condition was not actually achieved

---

# Minimum record fields
Keep it short and useful:

- **Condition**
- **Run count**
- **Measured parameter**
- **Limit**
- **Result**
- **Notes on drift / alarms / abnormal behavior**

---

# Quick structure to use on the floor

| Test type | Condition | Run count | Measured parameter | Limit | Result |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Reproduction | original trigger | 2 | failure signal | original threshold | pass/fail |
| Verification | expected range | 2–3 | key metric | spec limit | pass/fail |
| Soak | same stress, extended time | 1+ full dwell | drift indicator | no drift / no fault | pass/fail |
| Negative control | non-trigger condition | 1–2 | same metric | should stay normal | pass/fail |

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **fill-in-the-blanks one-page form** with:
- condition
- run count
- parameter
- limit
- pass/fail
- hold/stop box

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The envelope piece and the negative control are the part people usually hand-wave, and that’s exactly where bad conclusions sneak in. The separation into reproduction / verification / soak is cleaner too. I’d actually use that on the floor.

One small thing: for the acceptance criteria, I’d want the drift callout to be explicit, not just “abnormal behavior.” Put numbers on it if you can — delta, slope, band, whatever the machine actually gives you. Otherwise people start arguing after the fact.

But as a checklist base, this is solid.
